coredata.dc:description: Patulin (PAT) is a widespread fruit toxin. Trace-level PAT exposure can cause serious harm to human health. Herein, a multimodal PAT aptasensor was designed based on Ru(bpy)3 2+-based metal organic framework composited hydrogel (RuMOF@hydrogel) and versatile banana peel-derived carbonized polymer dots (BPPDs). RuMOF@hydrogel modified magnetic-electrode exhibited excellent anodic and cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission and stability. Meanwhile, the BPPDs could enhance anodic ECL of RuMOF@hydrogel, and also show excellent fluorescence (FL) and photothermal (PT) properties. With the aid of PAT-triggered hybridization chain reaction and magnetic separation, ECL, FL, and PT responses could be recorded concurrently. The detection limit can reach as low as 0.25 fg mL−1. The ratiometric ECL quantitation ensured the sensitivity and accuracy of this assay. And visual FL and portable PT modes contributed to the utility. Furthermore, this aptasensor demonstrated better performances than HPLC in fruit products and the protocol can be extended to determine various contaminants in foods.

head.ce:title: A novel multimodal aptasensor for Patulin detection in fruit products based on high-performance RuMOF@hydrogel and versatile pericarp-derived carbonized polymer dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of the Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of the Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of the Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Patulin (PAT) is a widespread fruit toxin. Trace-level PAT exposure can cause serious harm to human health. Herein, a multimodal PAT aptasensor was designed based on Ru(bpy)3 2+-based metal organic framework composited hydrogel (RuMOF@hydrogel) and versatile banana peel-derived carbonized polymer dots (BPPDs). RuMOF@hydrogel modified magnetic-electrode exhibited excellent anodic and cathodic electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission and stability. Meanwhile, the BPPDs could enhance anodic ECL of RuMOF@hydrogel, and also show excellent fluorescence (FL) and photothermal (PT) properties. With the aid of PAT-triggered hybridization chain reaction and magnetic separation, ECL, FL, and PT responses could be recorded concurrently. The detection limit can reach as low as 0.25 fg mL−1. The ratiometric ECL quantitation ensured the sensitivity and accuracy of this assay. And visual FL and portable PT modes contributed to the utility. Furthermore, this aptasensor demonstrated better performances than HPLC in fruit products and the protocol can be extended to determine various contaminants in foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Patulin (PAT), a fungal secondary metabolite, is also an exogenous harmful residue, has been reported to be present in fruits, vegetables, cereals, nuts, herbs, and even infant food (Sohrabi et al., 2022). Notably, fruits and related products are the most susceptible to the contamination, and PAT is therefore called the famous fruit toxin. Studies have shown that exposure to PAT may cause certain acute and chronic toxicity problems to human body, including gastrointestinal neurosis, neurotoxicity, immunotoxicity, teratogenicity, and carcinogenicity (Ngolong Ngea et al., 2020). Given the acute and long-term toxicity of PAT, China, the United States (US), the European Union (EU), and other countries and regions have set the maximum limit of PAT in fruit products at 50 μg kg−1. And EU have set the limit of PAT in infant food to 10 μg kg−1. According to the survey studies and conclusions of Vidal et al. and Bacha et al., the world's people are exposing to PAT contamination (Bacha et al., 2023; Vidal et al., 2019). As global consumption of fruits and their products increases, the focus should not only on exceedance issue, but also the bioaccumulative effects of trace PAT. Therefore, sensitive and accurate monitoring of PAT is of great vital to ensure food safety and human health.

At present, the conventional methods for the detection of PAT mainly include instrumental methods demonstrated by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Yu & Fan, 2017) and HPLC coupling techniques (e.g., HPLC-MS) (Zhao et al., 2019) and immunoassays represented by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) (Przybylska et al., 2021) and immunochromatography. The instrumental method has high analysis accuracy but has weaknesses in terms of equipment popularity, analysis speed, and sample pre-treatment, which is generally used as a confirmatory method. Immunoassay shows great advantages in rapid (qualitative or semi-quantitative) analysis of mycotoxins, but in practice, it has limitations such as large batch-to-batch variation, poor stability, and low sensitivity. Recently, biosensing assays have also been developed to detect PAT. For instance, combining with molecular imprinting technique, a surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensor was established for PAT detection and applied to apple juice sample (Çimen et al., 2022). And a fast PAT screening method based on Mn (OAP) Cl modified screen-printed carbon electrode was proposed later (Negut et al., 2023). But the usual single-signal quantitative mode is susceptible to the detection environment and equipment, and the reliability is insufficient. To tackle above issues, ratiometric sensors that rely on the ratio of dual outcoming signals, including fluorescent (Deng et al., 2022), electrochemical (Liu et al., 2024), and electrochemical-photoelectrochemical (dual model) sensor (Liu et al., 2023) have also been reported, which can effectively overcome the influence of external environmental disturbance on analytical results. A balance between analysis speed and detection accuracy can be achieved via biosensors. However, in order to cope with complex food substrates and restricted application scenarios, a sensitive, accurate, and utility biosensing assay of analyzing PAT should be achieved concurrently.

Ratiometric electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensors are gradually attracting the interest of researchers, due to its inherent advantages of low background interference, high sensitivity, and space-time controllability. Notably, the development of ratiometric ECL assay based on a single ECL luminophore is an interesting direction, as it avoids the harsh and complex process of double luminophore selection and design, greatly simplifying the experimental process (Yang et al., 2022). Tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) and its derivatives, with the superior characteristics of high quantum efficiency, good electrochemical reversibility, and anodic and cathodic ECL emission upon the regulation of coreactants and potential are the most used single luminophore by researchers. For example, Ju's group prepared Ru(bpy)3 2+ functionalized zinc-based metal-organic framework nanoflowers and achieved dual-potential ECL response to neuron-specific enolase when triethylamine (TEA) as anodic coreactant and potassium peroxydisulfate (K2S2O8) as cathodic coreactant (Dong et al., 2021). Needle-like nanostructures of Ru(bpy)3 2+ were synthesized to simultaneously generate dual-potential emissions based on black phosphorus quantum dots as anodic coreactant and K2S2O8 as cathodic coreactant and were utilized to detect tumor marker mucin 1 (Yin et al., 2021). Recently, our group first constructed a ratiometric ECL aptasensor based on dual-potential ECL emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped trimetallic nanocube for PAT detection, employing prepared anthocyanin-derived carbon quantum dots as anodic coreactant and K2S2O8 as cathodic coreactant, and achieved low LOD of 0.05 pg mL−1 (Lv et al., 2023). Due to the exorbitant price and excellent water solubility, the combination of the above research, it is reasonable to believe that the stable immobilization of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivatives on electrode surfaces is the future development orientation. Moreover, the optimal detection conditions of the two types of coreactants are different, and the coexistence of coreactants may interfere with the activity of the biomolecules and the stability of the assay. Thus, a simple and efficient Ru(bpy)3 2+-based ECL system need to be established.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) with large specific surface area, high porosity, and good electrical conductivity have been considered as an excellent pick to load Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Zhang, Gao, et al., 2023). However, the poor water stability of most MOFs makes it prone to skeleton collapse, leading to leakage of ECL luminophore and poor stability of biosensors. Therefore, improvements are needed for MOFs. Recently, Xiao's group synthesized a Ru-complex-based metal-covalent organic framework (Ru-MCOF) using tris(4,40-dicarboxylicacid-2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+) as the building block, which was stabler than MOFs and had more active sites than covalent organic frameworks (COFs) (Yang et al., 2023). Inspired by this innovative train of thought, we started to explore a new kind of MOFs-based composites. Hydrogel is a class of micro-nano material with high water content and three-dimensional network structure formed by physical or chemical crosslinking of polymers, which can be endowed with various functions by adjusting their composition and structural composition (Zhao et al., 2021). Due to their domain-limiting effect, they can effectively overcome the problem of energy loss due to collisions between ECL luminophores (Ding et al., 2019). However, weak mechanical properties are the main problem that hinders its development and application. Due to their superior properties compared to single MOF and hydrogel materials, MOFs-based hydrogel has been developed and applied in sensors (Zhang, Peng, et al., 2023), wound dressing (Chen et al., 2023), and water treatment (Song et al., 2023), and so on. To date, as far as we know, MOFs-based hydrogels have not been reported to be used in the construction of ECL biosensors. The authors believe that the innovative combination of Ru(bpy)3 2+-based MOF (RuMOF) and hydrogels will greatly improve the analytical performances of ECL biosensors.

It is advisable to employ both annihilation and coreaction mechanism to simplify the ECL system. Therefore, we attempt for the first time to achieve anodic and cathodic ECL emission of Ru(bpy)3 2+ relying only on anodic coreactants. Classical anodic coreactants represented by organic molecule such as tripropylamine tripropylamine (TPrA) and TEA, suffer from the inherent disadvantages of high toxicity and volatility. Due to the abundant surface active sites and excellent biocompatibility, various carbon dots (CDs) such as carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) (Li et al., 2022), carbon nanodots (C-dots) (Long et al., 2014), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (Qi et al., 2017) have been reported as anodic nano-coreactants of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivatives. Recently, a new type of CDs, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), that inherit the excellent properties of both CDs and polymers have entered the limelight. CPDs are often obtained through bottom-up approaches, and the choice of precursors can be small molecules, polymers, or even biomass. The abundance of carbon sources and the variety of synthesis strategies give CPDs various structures and properties (Ðorđević et al., 2022). Regrettably, at present, there is no report of CPDs as the coreactant. In this research, we proposed for the first time to use pericarp and branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI) as precursors to prepare CPDs. It was utilized as nano-coreactant, and exhibited excellent optical and photothermal properties.

Nonnegligibly, clever and efficient sensing strategy is a key way to improve the sensitivity, accuracy, and portability of biosensors. The hybridization chain reaction (HCR) is an efficient self-assembling nucleic acid amplification process that is simple, mild, and enzyme-free and is widely used for the detection of mycotoxins (Zhu et al., 2022). However, in conventional biosensors, the initiating DNA strands are usually immobilized on the electrode, which results in a cumbersome coupling process and low reaction rates. With magnetic separation, the initiating DNA strands are immobilized on magnetic nanoparticles and the HCR occurs in a homogeneous solution. The reaction products are then magnetically drawn to the electrode surface using a magnetic electrode, which eliminates the need for active coupling groups at the electrode modification interface and increases hybridization efficiency. More appealingly, both the magnetic complex and the supernatant produced after magnetic separation can be a source of signal, thus providing researchers with the technical facility to corroborate determination results. The point to emphasize here is that no dual-mode or multimode biosensors containing ratiometric ECL have been used for the detection of PAT or even other mycotoxins. Additionally, limited by the nature of the signal labels and the sensing strategies, most sensors are based on the same technology (e.g. fluorescence/colorimetry or electrochemistry/ECL). While the results mutually validate each other, enhancing reliability, the true mutual complementation and support of intertechnologies have not yet been fully realized.

In this experiment, we constructed a novel aptasensor and achieved ratiometric ECL, fluorescence (FL), and photothermal (PT) multimodes for PAT detection, by employing RuMOF composited hydrogel (RuMOF@hydrogel) as a single luminophore and banana peel-derived CPDs (BPPDs) as anodic nano-coreactant, fluorescent and photothermal labels. Firstly, as shown in Scheme 1A, RuMOF@hydroge-modified magnetic electrode yielded from a simple photo-induced polymerization allowed cathodic and anodic ECL emission under the potential range of −1.2 − +1.4 V. Secondly, by hydrothermal method, banana peel as a carbon source, combined with the polymer precursor BPEI, banana peel-derived CPDs (BPPDs) were synthesized. Then amino-functionalized SiO2-doped BPPDs (BPPDs@SiO2) were prepared by the reverse microemulsion method to achieve the enrichment of BPPDs and were used to label hairpin DNA (BPPDs@SiO2-HP) as signal probes (Scheme 1B). As shown in Scheme 1C, in the presence of PAT, the HCR reaction was triggered and a large number of BPPDs@SiO2-HP bound to gold nanoparticle loaded Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MAu). After magnetic separation, the HCR complex was introduced to the electrode surface, resulting in a significant enhancement of the anodic ECL and a weak reduction of the cathodic ECL, allowing quantitative determination PAT through the ECL signal ratio of anode and cathode. Meanwhile, due to the unbound BPPDs@SiO2-HP, the fluorescence and temperature of the supernatant were inversely proportional to the content of PAT. Thus, precision ratiometric ECL quantification combined with visual FL and portable PT modes was achieved in one sensing system, and opened a new chapter in the detection of PAT and even other contaminants.

All the oligonucleotides utilized in this research were ordered from Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and the oligonucleotide sequences are listed in Table S1. Patulin (PAT), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), ochratoxin (OTA) and zearalenone (ZEN) were provided by J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tri (2,2′-bipyridine) ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate (Ru(bpy)3 2+, 99.95%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). Branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI, M.W. 600, 99%), sodium alginate (SA), acrylamide (AM), N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA), and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were purchased from Aladdin Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium persulfate (APS) was bought form Meryer Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Pre-prepared solutions are as follows. Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH = 7.4) was prepared for oligonucleotides storage and reaction. PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4) used as the dilution buffer. PBS buffer (10 mM, pH = 8.0) abbreviated as PBSECL for ECL measurement. [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution (5 mM, 0.1 M KCl) was prepared for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) detection.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emission was performed on a model MPI-E ECL analyzer (Xi'an Remex Electronics Co. Ltd., Xi'an, China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were carried out by a CHI 660D analyzer (Chenhua Corp., China). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were acquired using Talos F200X (FEI., USA). The field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images were performed using S-4800 FESEM (JEOL., Japan). The fluorescence photograph was recorded using IX71-F22FL/PH inverted fluorescence microscope (IFM) (Olympus, Japan), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using the FTIR spectrometer (JASCO, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) data were collected using a PHI-5300 ESCA analyzer (PHI, America). (iMarkTM, Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyse was characterized by an Escalab 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, Scientific, United States).
Referring to previous reports, Ru(bpy)3 2+-based metal organic framework (RuMOF) was prepared with appropriate modifications (Supporting Information).

First, 0.01 g SA was mixed with 10 mL RuMOF solution (1 mg mL−1) under stirring conditions. Then 0.6 g AM, 0.01 g APS, and 0.02 g MBA were added in sequence to the above solution, as functional monomer, initiator, and crosslinker, respectively. After stirring and dissolving, the mixture was ultrasonically reacted in an ice water bath for 15 min to remove the bubbles, and the RuMOF@hydrogel precursor was obtained. As a control, Ru@hydrogel precursor without adding SA and Ru(bpy)3 2+ aqueous solution instead of RuMOF solution was prepared synchronously.

3 μL hydrogel precursor was drop-casted on a clean magnetic glassy carbon electrode (MGE) and was exposed to blue light for 5 min to obtain the hydrogel film. Then, it was soaked in deionized water for 30 min to remove the unreacted ingredients. After drying at room temperature, RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE was obtained and employed as the working electrode for further analysis. Based on the same steps, RuMOF/MGE and Ru@hydrogel/MGE were prepared as control.

Banana peels and BPEI-passivated carbon polymer dots (BPPDs) were prepared by hydrothermal method. The collected banana peels were dried and crushed into powder. Then 2 g obtained powder and 0.1 g BPEI were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water, mixed ultrasonically, and then transferred to reaction kettle at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a filter paper to remove larger precipitates, and the filtrate was later transferred into a dialysis tube for 48 h. Finally, after freeze-drying, the BPPDs powder was obtained. For control, apple peels, pineapple peels, and lychee peels were also collected and the corresponding carbon polymer dots were prepared.

Amino-functionalized silica-doped BPPDs (BPPDs@SiO2) were synthesized by the reverse microemulsion method to achieve the enrichment of BPPDs. The detailed preparation procedure is provided in the Supporting information.

Before use, the hairpin probes (HP) containing HP1 and HP2 were heated at 95 °C for 5 min and slowly cooled to room temperature. In detail, 50 μM HP1 and HP2 were mixed with 1 mL of prepared BPPDs@SiO2 (1 mg mL−1) with shaking for 30 min. Based on electrostatic adsorption, after centrifugation and washing, BPPDs@SiO2 labeled HP (BPPDs@SiO2-HP) was obtained. The obtained BPPDs@SiO2-HP are stored at 4 °C for further use.

20 μL of cDNA (10 μM) was added into 2 mL gold nanoparticle loaded Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MAu) solution (1 mg mL−1, detailed preparation process and characterization were provided in the Supporting information). After reacting at 4 °C overnight under gentle shaking, the MAu-cDNA was obtained by AuN bond, which was then blocked with BSA (1 mM) at 4 °C for 1 h. Then the obtained MAu-cDNA was redispersed in 2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer to incubate with 20 μL of Apt (5 μM) at 37 °C for 40 min with gentle stirring. After magnetic separation and washing, the obtained MAu-dsDNA was redispersed in 2 mL of Tris-HCl buffer for later use. Then a 50 μL aliquot of PAT-containing solution was mixed with 500 μL of the MAu-dsDNA and incubated at 37 °C for another 40 min. Subsequently, 100 μL mixture of BPPDs@SiO2-HP1 and BPPDs@SiO2-HP2 was added to the above solution and reacted at 37 °C for 2 h. Finally, under the effect of an external magnetic field, the supernatant containing unreacted BPPDs@SiO2-HP1 and BPPDs@SiO2-HP2 was transferred for further fluorescence (FL) and photothermal (PT) mode measurement. And the obtained magnetically controlled bioconjugations were collected again and redispersed in 2 mL PBS buffer for further measurement.

RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE was inserted into the HCR amplification solution and left for 3 min to allow the magnetic bioconjugations to be fully adsorbed onto the electrode surface. The modified electrode was then washed and immersed in PBSECL for further ECL detection. All ECL experiments were measured with a three-electrode system by using MGE as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) electrode as the reference electrode, and platinum wire as the counter electrode. ECL measurement potential was set from −1.8 to +1.2 V with a scan rate of 100 mV/s. The photomultiplier voltage is set at 700 V. I ECL-A represents the ECL intensity of the anode and I ECL-c represents the ECL intensity of the cathode. The ratio of I ECL-A and I ECL-C (I ECL-A/I ECL-C) was calculated.
The fluorescence intensity of supernatant samples at 440 nm was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the spectra scanned from 400 to 700 nm. And ΔI FL (I FL- I 0 FL) was counted.
The temperature of supernatant samples was recorded with irradiation 10 min using an 808 nm laser (0.6 W cm −2). And ΔT (T - T0) was calculated.
All measurement results were repeated 3 times.

Fresh fruits, fruit products and infant foods, including apples, hawthorn, peaches, dried apple, preserved hawthorn, canned peach, puree, jam, and apple juice, were collected from fruit markets and infant supermarkets in Nanjing. Each species was randomly purchased 500 g each time. After five random purchases, a total of forty-five samples were collected. Samples of the same species were crushed and mixed homogeneously and divided into three equal parts. The samples were pre-treated according to the previous report (Liu et al., 2023). Apple juice samples were diluted directly with PBS buffer. 5 g solid and semisolid samples were dissolved respectively in 25 mL ethyl acetate. The mixtures were centrifuged at 6000 r/min for 5 min, and the supernatants were collected and dried in a 40 °C water bath with nitrogen. Then, the resulting powders were dissolved in 1 mL of acetonitrile. Finally, the PAT concentration of the samples was measured employing this multimodal assay and HPLC, and each sample was measured three times in parallel.

As shown in Fig. 1A, after 5 min of exposure under blue light, when the container was inverted, the Ru-MOF@hydrogel had no tendency to flow, indicating the formation of a hydrogel state. And the RuMOF@hydrogel could be prepared in different shapes according to requirements and keep the properties of Ru-MOF (Fig. 1B). The micromorphologies of RuMOF, Ru@hydrogel, and RuMOF@hydrogel were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM). In Fig. 1C, RuMOF presented a clear octahedral structure with a rough surface and uniform size distribution with an average diameter of about 200 nm. Fig. S1 showed that Ru@hydrogel had a typical honeycomb structure with uniform pore distribution, indicating a uniform polymerization reaction of AM. And the inverted fluorescence microscope (IFM) image of Ru@hydrogel (the inset in Fig. S1) exhibited orange fluorescence, meaning successful doping of Ru(bpy)3 2+. When RuMOF and SA were doped into the hydrogel precursor solution, the prepared RuMOF@hydrogel showed a 3D porous structure with parallel layered (Fig. 1D), and the pore distribution was more uniform and the pore size was smaller. This reflected that the semi-interpenetrating network was formed evenly between SA and polymerized AM (PAM), and the formation of hydrogen bond between RuMOF and SA. Moreover, according to the local magnification diagram (Fig. 1E), a large amount of RuMOF was observed to be loaded in the pores of hydrogel, displaying that hydrogel is an excellent carrier of RuMOF. And a much brighter orange fluorescence 3D hole structure was presented in the inverted fluorescence microscope (IFM) image of RuMOF@hydrogel (inset in Fig. 1E). Meanwhile, in Fig. 1F, the responding distribution of C, O, N, Na, Zr, and Ru elements were recorded by SEM-energy disperse spectroscopy (SEM-EDS) mapping, which further proved the uniform distribution of RuMOF and the successful synthesis of RuMOF@hydrogel. Moreover, the surface structure of RuMOF@hydrogel was characterized by FT-IR. As can be seen from Fig. S2, the spectral lines of RuMOF@hydrogel showed characteristic peaks of RuMOF, MA, and SA, indicating the successful synthesis of RuMOF@hydrogel (Liu, Li, et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and property research of BPPDs and BPPDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, we compared the optical and ECL enhancement properties of litchi rind-derived CPDs (LRPDs), pineapple peel-derived CPDs (PAPDs), apple peel-derived CPDs (APPDs), and banana peel-derived CPDs (BPPDs). All four pericarp raw materials could be dried and pulverized to a uniform fine powder (Fig. S3A). The APPDs, PAPDs, LRPDs, and BPPDs all showed blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. S3B). In addition, we compared the anodic ECL response of RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE in PBSECL containing 2 mg mL−1 LRPDs, PAPDs, APPDs, and BPPDs, respectively. The anodic ECL of Ru-MOF@hydrogel/MGE was enhanced to different degrees by different pericarp-derived CPDs, with the BPPDs showing the best enhancement (Fig. S4). This may be due to the differences in the composition of carbohydrates, proteins, fibers, and minerals contained in the different peels. Due to banana peels being easy to collect and having a good ECL enhancement effect, banana peels were chosen as the carbon source for this research.

The microstructure of BPPDs and BPPDs@SiO2 were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2A, BPPDs exhibit uniform quasispherical shapes with a narrow size distribution from 1 nm to 7 nm. In the inset of Fig. 2A, an obvious crystalline structure was not observed in most of the nanoparticles, indicating BPPDs were not completely graphitized (Xia et al., 2018). The TEM image of BPPDs@SiO2 manifested as a spherical homogenized structure with an average diameter of 300 nm (Fig. 2B). Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR) spectroscopy was employed to reveal the chemical structures of BPPDs and BPPDs@SiO2. As shown in curve a of Fig. 2C, the peak at 1620 cm−1 is the stretching vibration characteristic peak of the carbonyl group (C=O), the wide peak at 3600–3200 cm−1 is the stretching vibration peak of hydroxyl group (OH) and amino group (NH), and the peaks at 2930 and 2870 cm−1 are the two characteristic peaks of methylene group (-CH2) that derived from BPEI (Xia et al., 2018). In curve b, in addition to retaining the characteristic absorption peak of BPPDs, the strong absorption peak at 1070 cm−1 is the asymmetric stretching vibration peak of Si-O-Si, which proves the successful synthesis of BPPDs@SiO2 (Ibrahim et al., 2023). By measuring UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra, we further investigated the optical properties of BPPDs and BPPDs@SiO2. As can be seen from Fig. 2D (dot-dash line), BPPDs@SiO2 shows a wide absorption peak at 360 nm (curve a’), which is in good agreement with BPPDs (curve a). As shown in Fig. S5, the maximum emission and intensity of BPPDs change with the increase of excitation wavelength, which indicates the excitation-dependent emission behavior of BPPDs. And under ultraviolet irradiation, BPPDs and BPPDs@SiO2 appeared as bright blue luminescence, which was attributed to the molecular state, carbon core and surface states, and cross-linking-enhanced emission mechanisms of BPPDs (Tao et al., 2022). In Fig. 2D (solid line), at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, compared with BPPDs (curve a), due to the surface effect of the silica shell, the FL emission peak of BPPDs@SiO2 redshifted from 440 nm to 460 nm. Therefore, 360 nm and 440 nm were chosen as optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of BPPDs@SiO2, respectively. BPEI with branched chains containing primary, secondary and tertiary amines, has been proven to be an effective coreactant for Ru(bpy)3 2+ and its derivatives. And we compared the anodic ECL enhancement effects of BPEI, TPrA, BPPDs, and BPPDs@SiO2. The concentration of BPPDs, BPPDs@SiO2, and BPEI is 2 mg mL−1, the concentration of TPrA is 5 mM, and the concentration of Ru(bpy)3 2+ is 1 mM. As shown in Fig. 2E, when Ru(bpy)3 2+ was absent and only BPPDs (curve a) or BPPDs@SiO2 (curve b) existed, no ECL signals were generated. And just weak ECL response was obtained from Ru(bpy)3 2+ (curve c). Whereas, strong ECL emission was observed in the system where Ru(bpy)3 2+ was co-existing with BPEI (d), BPPDs (e), TPrA (f), and BPPDs@SiO2 (g), respectively. This may be due to that BPPDs retain a large number of amine-rich fragments on the surface, derived from the BPEI precursor, which provides the co-reactive groups (Xiong et al., 2017). The anodic ECL enhancement of BPPDs@SiO2 for Ru(bpy)3 2+ was about twice as effective as BPPDs, which was ascribed to the enrichment of silicon. The results also proved our prepared BPPDs@SiO2 could produce similar enhancement effects with commercial TPrA. Moreover, as Fig. 2F illustrated, under the irradiation of 808 nm laser, the temperature increment in ultrapure water was negligible (curve a). However, we noticed that after 600 s of irradiation, the temperature of BPPDs aqueous solution (2 mg mL−1) increased by 23.5 °C (curve b). And the temperature of BPPDs@SiO2 aqueous solution (2 mg mL−1) increased by 28.2 °C at the same condition, which is mainly attributed to that the SiO2 shell suppresses light reflection and improves light absorption (Wu & Tracy, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL and self-healing performance of RuMOF@hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the micromorphology, we seem to conclude that hydrogel is an excellent carrier of ECL emitter. To verify the superiority of RuMOF@hydrogel, the anodic ECL intensities of RuMOF/MGE, Ru@hydrogel/MGE, and RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE were measured in PBSECL containing 2 mg mL−1 BPPDs@SiO2 during potential from 0 to +1.4 V. As can be seen from the Fig. 3A, a decaying ECL response was observed from RuMOF/MGE (curve a). This may be due to the absence of force between RuMOF and MGE. Similarly, a slightly stronger and gradually decaying ECL response was recorded from Ru@hydrogel/MGE (curve b), this indicated that Ru(bpy)3 2+ participated in the polymerization of hydrogels and was successfully doped. However, due to the lack of specific binding force between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and monomers, there was a leakage problem. While a strong and stable ECL response was presented from RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE (curve c), and relative standard deviations (RSD) of ECL signal values generated within 300 s is only 2.94%. On the one hand, RuMOF was bound to SA by electrostatic action and fixed in the hydrogel network. On the other hand, it is the 3D porous structure of the hydrogel that facilitated rapid mass transfer and electron transfer within the pore channel, which could improve the efficiency of electrochemical and ECL reactions (Nishat et al., 2022). Further, after soaking the RuMOF@hydrogel in PBS buffer for 10 h, only a weak ECL intensity was recorded in the supernatant solution (Fig. S6), which was attributed to the domain-limiting role of 3D porous structure of the hydrogel (Ding et al., 2019) and making Ru not easy to leak. It should be noted that physical damage to the modification electrode surface would also affect the ECL response. The application of modification materials with self-healing properties may greatly improve the long-term stability of the ECL system (Han & Guo, 2020). At room temperature, the hydrogel was tested to explore whether it could be restored by the intrinsic properties after being damaged. As Fig. 3B shows, with no external energy input, the broken hydrogel had healed as normal and could withstand tensile forces. This indicates that hydrogel has good self-healing properties, which is attributed to the hydrogen bonding within hydrogels (Zhao et al., 2021). Moreover, the ECL emission loss after hydrogel destruction also recovered gradually during the hydrogel self-healing process (Fig. 3C). By 10 min, the ECL intensity had recovered to >90% of the initial signal intensity. The above results prove that RuMOF@hydrogel can be considered an excellent ECL material and electrode modification material.

In order to construct a dual-potential ratiometric aptasensor, we investigated the effect of BPPDs@SiO2 on the cathodic and anodic ECL of RuMOF@hydrogel and the reaction mechanism (Fig. 4A). As depicted in the lower part of Fig. 4A (lower part), when BPPDs@SiO2 exist alone, neither cathodic nor anodic ECL was generated (curve a). When there are no BPPDs@SiO2 in the detection system, due to the annihilation pathway, weak cathodic and anodic ECL response of RuMOF@hydrogel was observed (curve b) (Cao et al., 2020). While after BPPDs@SiO2 was introduced, the anodic ECL signal significantly enhanced and the cathodic signal slightly decreased, and the response potential did not change (curve c), indicating that BPPDs@SiO2 could effectively improve the anodic ECL performance of RuMOF@hydrogel and only act as anodic coreactant of RuMOF@hydrogel. The slight decline in cathodic ECL might contributed to the competitive-mechanism-driven of dual-potential ECL for a single ECL emitter (curve b). In the upper part of Fig. 4A, the CV curves were displayed to further explore the role of BPPDs@SiO2. In the absence of BPPDs@SiO2, a pair of reversible redox peaks of RuMOF@hydrogel was observed (curve d). And BPPDs@SiO2 showed no redox peak (curve e). While upon the addition of BPPDs@SiO2, the oxidation current of RuMOF@hydrogel increased significantly (curve f). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of BPPDs@SiO2, RuMOF@hydrogel, and a mixture of both was determined to further elucidate the ECL mechanism of RuMOF@hydrogel/BPPDs@SiO2 ECL system (Fig. 4B). BPPDs@SiO2 has an internal absorption peak at 375 nm (curve a), and RuMOF@hydrogel has two absorption peaks at 285 nm and 452 nm (curve b), which can be attributed to π-π∗ transition, metal centered transition, and metal-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) (Yan et al., 2019). After mixing RuMOF@hydrogel and BPPDs@SiO2 (curve c), the location of each characteristic peak axis did not change, indicating that no chemical reaction occurred in the ground state (Liu, Xu, et al., 2021). And the stability of this system was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4C, after 10 consecutive scans, the anotic and cathodic ECL response of RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE was basically unchanged in PBSECL containing 2 mg mL−1 BPPDs@SiO2 during potential from −1.8 to +1.4 V.

Based on the above discussion and research, we propose that there are two possible ECL routes. RuMOF is the only ECL active substance in this system, BPPDs are the nano-coreactants, hydrogels and silica act as carriers of RuMOF and BPPDs for fixation and enrichment respectively, which do not participate in the ECL reaction process, so the ECL reaction equation only involves RuMOF and BPPDs. The first is the annihilation route, where (RuMOFs)2+ was oxidized and reduced at the electrode surface to produce (RuMOFs)3+ (eq. 1) and (RuMOFs)+, respectively (eq. 2). (RuMOFs)3+ and (RuMOFs)+ could continue to generate excited states (RuMOF)2+⁎ by the electron-transfer annihilation process (eq. 3). Finally, the excited states emit ECL when they leap like ground states (eq. 4).

The other is the coreaction route. In this system, BPPDs were introduced as anodic coreactants. Based on the studies of the ECL performance of RuMOFs@/BPPDs, referring to the ECL mechanism of Ru(bpy)2 3+-TPrA, it was speculated that the pathway was as follows. First, (RuMOFs)2+ was oxidized to (RuMOFs)3+ (eq. 5), and BPPDs could lose electrons and be oxidized to BPPDs·+ (eq. 6). Subsequently, BPPDs·+ would deprotonate to form intermediates BPPDs· (eq. 7). Then (RuMOFs)3+ combined with BPPDs· to form an excited (RuMOFs)3+∗ (eq. 8). Eventually, (RuMOFs)3+∗ underwent a radiative transition to the ground state (RuMOFs)2+, emitting light in the process (eq. 9).

Moreover, the weak decrease of cathode signal may be due to the competition mechanism between the annihilation route and the coreaction route of RuMOFs (Song et al., 2023). Since the coreaction route could occur at lower potentials with higher efficiency, with more (RuMOFs)3+ participation. Therefore, fewer radicals were generated and involved in the annihilation pathway, resulting in a reduced cathode signal. The schematic diagram of the coreaction and annihilation pathway of ECL system are shown in Fig. 4D.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility testing and electrochemical characterization and of the proposed aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Agarose gel electrophoresis (AGE, mass fraction 3%) was first employed to characterize and validate HCR product and PAT-triggered HCR systems, and the results are shown in Fig. S7A. Consistent with the verification results in our previous reports (Lv et al., 2023), double strands DNA (dsDNA) were formed between cDNA and aptamer (line 1). When cDNA, HP1, and HP2 were incubated together, a slower electrophoresis band with wide dispersion was observed in lane 2, indicating that cDNA could open HP1 and HP2 consecutively for formation of long dsDNA by HCR, but there is no spontaneous HCR between dsDNA and HP1 + HP2 (lane 3). However, when PAT was added to the above system and bonded to aptamer, a slow and dispersed band similar to lane 2 appeared in lane 4. These results indicated that PAT could trigger HCR amplification in this system, as a result of the exposure of cDNA. Moreover, the bioconjugations (HCR products-MAu) could be magnetically adsorbed by MGE (Fig. S7B), meaning that employing magnetic separation technology is feasible.

EIS was selected as the systematic monitoring tool for the stepwise modification of the ratiometric aptasensor. As shown in Fig. 5A, electron transfer resistance (Ret) was reflected through the diameter of the semicircle using [Fe (CN)6]3−/4 as the redox probe. Compared with bare MGE (curve a), Ret of the RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE slightly decreased (curve b). It is attributed to the increased electrode surface active area and good electrical conductivity of RuMOF. Ret value gradually decreased when MAu-cDNA (curve c), MAu-BSA (curve d), and MAu-dsDNA (curve e) were introduced into RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE by magnetic force, owing to the electrostatic repulsion between Fe (CN)6 4−/3− and the negative charges of DNA phosphoric acid backbone, and the blocking effect of BSA to electron transfer. After PAT carried the aptamer away from the electrode surface, the Ret value was reduced (curve f). Subsequently, the occurrence of HCR led to an anabatic value of Ret (curve g), implying the formation of a long double-stranded HCR product.

The corresponding ECL, FL, and PT responses of the multimodal aptasensor to PAT were recorded to verify the feasibility. As shown in Fig. 5B, no obvious change in cathode and anode ECL intensity was observed on dsDNA/MGE (curve a). However, when 1 pg mL−1 PAT was added and triggered the HCR, a large amount of BPPDs@SiO2 was introduced into the electrode surface by forming long dsDNA structures, leading to cathodic ECL depressed slightly and anodic ECL increased strongly (curve b). This illustrated that the proposed aptasensor could be applied to implement ratiometric ECL for PAT assay. Simultaneously, in Fig. 5C and Fig. 5D, when PAT was present in the reaction system, there was a large amount of unreacted BPPDs@SiO2-HP in the supernatant after magnetic separation, exhibiting strong fluorescence emission and high solution temperature (curve a). After 5 pg mL−1 PAT was added, plenty of BPPDs@SiO2-HP was introduced into the electrode surface through an HCR reaction triggered by PAT, resulting in the decline of fluorescence intensity and temperature of the supernatant (curve b). The proposed aptasensor can achieve PAT determination based on ratiometric ECL, FL, and PT modes. On the one hand, these three modes can verify each other to improve the reliability of detection results. On the other hand, the portability and visualization of FL and PT modes and the precision and sensitivity of ratiometric ECL can complement each other.

Several key parameters that may affect the analytical performance of the proposed aptasensor were optimized. It is noted that the amount of hydrogel modified on the electrode surface significantly affects the signal performance, so the hydrogel drop-casting volume was optimized. As shown in Fig. S8A, the ECL response intensity of RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE was highest when the drop-casting volume was 3 μL. Although the amount of RuMOF drawn to the electrode surface increased with the increase of the drop-casting volume, the too-thick hydrogel film may hinder the electron transfer, leading to a decreased ECL signal. Therefore, in this research, the hydrogel drop-casting volume was set as 3 μL. The pH of the PBSECL also affects the ECL response. As can be seen in Fig. S8B, the I ECL-A/I ECL-C maximum of RuMOF@hydrogel/MGE in PBSECL containing 2 mg mL−1 BPPDs was achieved when the pH was 8.0. The possible cause is that under alkaline conditions, BPPDs are easy to deprotonate and form reducing intermediates (BPPDs*) and then react with (RuMOFs)3+, leading to ECL emission (Xing et al., 2018). Thus, pH 8.0 was adopted for ECL detection. Moreover, HCR reaction time is also an important experimental condition. Fig. S8C indicated that the reaction time was between 2 h and 3 h, both I ECL-A/I ECL-C and ΔI FL reached the maximum, indicating that HCR reaction was complete at this period of time. In order to improve the detection efficiency, 2 h was selected as the HCR reaction time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL, FL, and PT response of the aptasensor to PAT
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal assay parameters, this proposed multimodal aptasensor was utilized to detect different concentrations of PAT by measuring ECL, FL, and temperature responses. Fig. 6A shows that as the concentrations of PAT increased (from 5 fg mL−1 to 0.5 ng mL−1), the anodic ECL increased gradually, and the cathodic ECL decreased slightly with the concentration change, which reflected the competence of the aptasensor in PAT quantification. The logarithmic value of I ECL-A/I ECL-C also presented a linear relationship with the logarithm of PAT between 0.5 fg mL−1 and 0.5 ng mL−1. And the calibration curve was lg I ECL-A/I ECL-C = −0.3256 + 0.2934 lg (CPAT (fg mL−1) (R = 0.998) (Fig. 6B). Both I ECL-A and I ECL-C showed a linear relationship with the logarithm of PAT from 5 fg mL−1 to 0.5 ng mL−1. The regression equation was I ECL-A = 2207.82 + 264.02 lg (CPAT (fg mL−1) (R = 0.992) and I ECL-C = 2518.09–383.97 lg (CPAT (fg mL−1) (R = 0.994) (Fig. S9A and 9B). It is worth noting that the ratiometric assay exhibited a more satisfying R value, demonstrating the great reliability of the ratiometric ECL biosensor. And the calculated limit of detection (LOD) was 0.25 fg mL−1. FL measurement was also employed to monitor the linear range and LOD for PAT quantification. As shown in Fig. 6C, as PAT concentration increased from 0 to 10 ng mL−1, the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant system decreased gradually and correspondingly, the fluorescence signal under UV lamp was gradually turned off (inset in Fig. 6E), which can be distinguished by the naked eyes. There was a good linear relationship between ΔI FL and the logarithm of PAT concentrations from 0.1 pg mL−1 to 1 ng mL−1. The linear regression equation was ΔI FL = 965.86 + 384.38 lg (CPAT (pg mL−1) (R = 0.993) (Fig. 6D). And the calculated LOD was 34 fg mL−1. Similarly, after irradiation 10 min of 808 nm laser, the temperature of the supernatant gradually decreased with the increase of PAT concentration (from 0.5 pg mL−1 to 5 ng mL−1) (Fig. 6E). The linear regression equation can be depicted as ΔT =13.15 + 5.95 lg (CPAT (pg mL−1) (R = 0.990) with a LOD 0.2 pg mL−1 (LOD = 3σb/slope, the σb on behalf of the standard deviation of eleven blank samples) (Fig. 6F). All the experiments were repeated three times.

The excellent ECL analysis performance is attributed to the high efficiency of hydrogel for RuMOF loading and the outstanding anodic ECL enhancement effect of BPPDs. The FL and PT mode depends not only on the clever magnetic separation strategy but also on the excellent FL and PT performances of BPPDs. As shown in Table S2, we compared the fabricated assay with PAT biosensing methods reported previously. The proposed multimodal aptasensor has a wider linear range. A lower LOD was achieved by ratiometric ECL mode. And FL provided the opportunity for visual analysis. Overall, the proposed multimodal protocol is a fascinating strategy, which is capable of achieving initial screening of PAT by the FL and PT modes and accurate quantifying of PAT by the ECL mode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, reproducibility, and stability of the multimodal aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the selectivity of the proposed multimodal assay, common mycotoxin AFB1, OTA, and ZEN were selected as interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 6G, I ECL-A/I ECL-C of HCR/MGE, I FL, and temperature of the supernatant obtained by incubating the aptasensor with 10 pg mL−1 AFB1, OTA, and ZEN were consistent with that obtained by blank. However, whether in PBS buffer or infant apple juice matrix, when the aptasensor was incubated with a mixture of AFB1, OTA, and ZEN, all three I ECL-A/I ECL-C, I FL, and temperature were similar to that of 1 pg mL−1 PAT. The results indicated that the prepared biosensor had the ability to discriminate the target analyte and other substances that may be present in the sample matrix. In addition, the reproducibility of the ECL mode was evaluated by preparing five different batches by different electrodes and on different days and measuring their responses towards 1 pg mL−1 PAT under the same conditions (Fig. 6H). RSD of intra and inter-assay were 2.72 and 3.38%, respectively. Moreover, we also explored the long-term stability of the aptasensor, the I ECL-A/I ECL-C, I FL, and temperature of supernatant were recorded once every five days. As shown in Fig. 6I, after 30 days, I ECL-A/I ECL-C, I FL, and temperature still retained >90% of the initial signal value, implying a reliable storage stability of the multimodal sensing platform.

To evaluate the feasibility of this protocol, by standard addition method, PAT-free infant apple juice samples were mixed with the known amount of standard PAT solution and were diluted with PBS buffer to obtain spiking samples. And the samples with 0.001, 5, 100, and 5000 pg mL−1 PAT were measured by this assay and golden standard-HPLC. Each sample was determined 3 times in parallel to calculate the corresponding recovery. As shown in Table S3, the recoveries of ECL, FL, and PT detection ranged from 89.2% to 110%, 86.4% to 113.4%, and 86.8% to 117.8%, respectively. And corresponding RSD was 2.35–4.86%, 3.29–6.44%, and 5.32–8.14, respectively, indicating that the aptasensor had good accuracy and repeatability in detecting PAT. Furthermore, a series of fresh fruits, fruit products and infant foods were tested by the multimodal assay and HPLC. As shown in Table 1 , except for fresh peaches and infant apple juice, other samples were detected with different degrees of PAT contamination. Among which, the content of PAT in fruit products was relatively high. Although the levels did not exceed the maximum limit of PAT, it should still be taken seriously. By paired t-test, the obtained results of the two methods were in good agreement (p > 0.05). Meanwhile, the above results confirmed that the ratiometric ECL mode has unique advantages in the detection of trace PAT in fresh fruits and baby foods, while the FL and PT mode enables rapid detection of high-concentration samples, which proves that this method has good practicability.

With PAT as the target, a novel aptasensor integrated precision ratiometric ECL quantification, visual FL, and portable PT modes was constructed based on RuMOF@hydrogel and BPPDs. RuMOF@hydrogel, as a novel ECL luminophore, was synthesized by simple photo-induced polymerization, and exhibited excellent anodic and cathodic ECL emission, domain-limiting effect, and good self-healing properties. BPPDs were prepared with banana peel as the carbon source and BPEI as the polymer precursor, which could effectively enhance the anodic ECL of RuMOF@hydrogel and also show excellent FL and PT properties. By employing PAT-triggered HCR and magnetic separation, the versatility of BPPDs was fully exploited and multimodal PAT detection was skillfully realized. The LODs of ECL, FL, and PT modes were 2.5 fg mL−1, 34 fg mL−1 and 0.2 pg mL−1, respectively, and satisfactory specificity, stability, and recovery were obtained. The ratiometric ECL mode ensured the glorious sensitivity and accuracy of this assay, and visual FL and portable PT modes contributed to the excellent analysis utility. Gratifyingly, the three modes can verify each other and can achieve a win-win situation in terms of the analysis performances. Notably, by flexibly designing the base sequences of the relevant oligonucleotides, the proposed aptasensor protocol can be extended to determine other food contaminants and provide a practical path for contaminant control in foodstuffs.

Liangrui Lv: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qin Tao: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Yijing Kuang: Validation, Methodology. Tingting Zhang: Data curation. Xiaoying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.
